title: Working with Remotes
learningObjectives: Add and manage remote connections with git remote
Understand the origin convention and how git clone sets it up
Distinguish between git fetch and git pull
Push branches and set upstream tracking with -u
Understand remote-tracking branches and what they represent

theory.content:
What is a Remote?

A remote is a named URL pointing to another copy of your repository — typically on a hosting service like GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket. Remotes let you synchronise history between your local repository and the shared one.

By convention, the primary remote is called origin. When you run git clone <url>, Git automatically creates an origin remote pointing to that URL. You can have multiple remotes (useful for forking workflows where you have both origin pointing to your fork and upstream pointing to the original project).

# List all remotes with their URLs
git remote -v

# Sample output:
# origin  https://github.com/alice/my-project.git (fetch)
# origin  https://github.com/alice/my-project.git (push)

Cloning vs Adding a Remote

There are two ways to connect a local repository to a remote:

  • git clone <url>: Downloads the entire repository history, creates a local copy, and automatically configures origin pointing to the cloned URL. The default branch is checked out.
  • git remote add origin <url>: For an existing local repository that was created with git init. Manually registers the remote so you can start pushing and pulling.
# For an existing local repo — add a remote
git remote add origin https://github.com/alice/my-project.git

# Rename a remote
git remote rename origin upstream

# Remove a remote
git remote remove upstream

# Change the URL of an existing remote
git remote set-url origin https://github.com/alice/new-name.git

Fetch vs Pull

This is one of the most important distinctions for safe collaborative workflows:

git fetch origin downloads all new objects (commits, branches, tags) from the remote and updates your remote-tracking branches (e.g., origin/main), but it does not modify your working directory or local branches. You can inspect the fetched changes before integrating them. This makes fetch safe to run at any time.

git pull is a convenience command that performs git fetch followed immediately by git merge (or git rebase if configured with --rebase). It integrates the remote changes into your current local branch in one step.

# Download new data without changing local branches
git fetch origin

# Inspect what arrived
git log origin/main --oneline

# Now merge when ready
git merge origin/main

# Or do both at once with pull
git pull

# Pull with rebase instead of merge
git pull --rebase

For explicit control, prefer fetch + review + merge over a bare git pull.

Pushing and Tracking Branches

To share your local commits with the remote, use git push:

# Push a branch to origin
git push origin main

# First push — set upstream tracking so future git push/pull need no args
git push -u origin main
# equivalent long form:
git push --set-upstream origin main

The -u (or --set-upstream) flag configures a tracking relationship: it records in .git/config that your local main branch tracks origin/main. After this, you can simply run git push or git pull with no arguments.

Remote-tracking branches like origin/main and origin/feature-x are read-only local references that reflect the last known state of the remote. They are updated automatically by git fetch (and by the fetch step inside git pull).

Authentication

Git supports two main authentication methods for HTTPS and SSH remotes:

  • HTTPS with Personal Access Token (PAT): Use your username and a PAT (not your account password) when prompted. Configure a credential helper to avoid re-entering credentials: git config --global credential.helper store (persistent) or cache (temporary).
  • SSH key pair: Generate a key with ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your@email.com", add the public key to GitHub/GitLab, and use SSH remote URLs (e.g., git@github.com:alice/my-project.git). SSH is recommended for frequent use as it avoids credential prompts entirely once set up.
